This is an illustrative case on how to use blastFoam with a simplechemistry. A single methane reaction is used, with two regions: The left containing 77% Nitrogen and 23& Oxygen at STP. The right region is pure methane at 10 atm and 500K. An Euler ODE solver for the chemistry reactions.

The calculation took approx. 30 s to run on a single core laptop at using 500 cells.

# Triple-Point Shock Interaction

## Notes

This case is a Riemann problem with three states and two materials, where the the domain is split into three separate regions. Each region has an ideal gas equation of state. This case is designed to test the ability to propagate shocks over a multi-material regions and capture the generation of vorticity.

The case takes approximately 37 min to run on a 4 core desktop.

- **Case Path**: solids4Foam/flap
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Simple FSI flaps simulation

## Notes

This case is used to demonstrate the capability of blastFoam to handle fluid-structure interaction.

The blastFSIFoam solver is still under development and changing the case setup may result in crashes.

The case consists of a small c4 charge being detonated near a wall in 2 dimensions. The flap will deform when the blast wave reached it, and will effect how the wave moves past the flap. This is a modified version of the preCICE tutorial presented.

The calculation took approx. 2 hrs to run on a single core laptop at the reference resolution (e.g. 240x160 cells) and a maximum refinement level of 1.

## Type: File (ID: blastFoam_reacting::blastFoam/reacting/0/CH4.orig)

### Case Context
- **Case Name**: reacting
- **Case Path**: blastFoam/reacting
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Reacting Shock tube

## Notes

This is an illustrative case on how to use blastFoam with a simplechemistry. A single methane reaction is used, with two regions: The left containing 77% Nitrogen and 23& Oxygen at STP. The right region is pure methane at 10 atm and 500K. An Euler ODE solver for the chemistry reactions.

The calculation took approx. 30 s to run on a single core laptop at using 500 cells.

## Type: File (ID: solids4Foam_flap::solids4Foam/flap/constant/fluid/turbulenceProperties)

### Case Context
- **Case Name**: flap
- **Case Path**: solids4Foam/flap
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Simple FSI flaps simulation

## Notes

This case is used to demonstrate the capability of blastFoam to handle fluid-structure interaction.

The blastFSIFoam solver is still under development and changing the case setup may result in crashes.

The case consists of a small c4 charge being detonated near a wall in 2 dimensions. The flap will deform when the blast wave reached it, and will effect how the wave moves past the flap. This is a modified version of the preCICE tutorial presented.

The calculation took approx. 2 hrs to run on a single core laptop at the reference resolution (e.g. 240x160 cells) and a maximum refinement level of 1.



### Node Properties
- **Name**: turbulenceProperties
- **Path**: solids4Foam/flap/constant/fluid/turbulenceProperties
- **Source_file**: solids4Foam_flap.json
- **Case_id**: solids4Foam/flap
- **Original_id**: solids4Foam/flap/constant/fluid/turbulenceProperties

### Case Context
- **Case Name**: movingCone
- **Case Path**: blastFoam/movingCone
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# movingCone tutorial

## Notes

This case is a modified version of the OpenFOAM movingCone tutorial. The main modification is the use of the movingAdaptiveFvMesh dynamic mesh class. This allows for automatic mesh refinement in addition to the mesh movement.

If you would like to run this case in parallel, the Synthetik branch of OpenFOAM must be used due to a bug that causes moving, adaptive meshes to fail in parallel.

The case takes approximately 15 minutes on a four core desktop.

## Type: File (ID: blastEulerFoam_shockTube_polydisperse::blastEulerFoam/shockTube_polydisperse/constant/turbulenceProperties.coal)

### Case Context
- **Case Name**: shockTube_polydisperse
- **Case Path**: blastEulerFoam/shockTube_polydisperse
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Validation of polydispserse granular

## Notes

This case uses six granular phases within a continuous air phase. This a dilute simulation where packing of particles does no occur. The continuous gas phase is represented by air. Five of the particles phases are used to represent a continuous size distribution with a density 2700 kg/m^3 and a coefficient of restitution of 0.9. The last granular phase is that of coal with a density of 1300 kg/m^3, coefficient of restitution of 0.9, and a diameter of 10e-6 m.

Original plots were digitized using WebPlotDigitizer: https://github.com/ankitrohatgi/WebPlotDigitizer/releases The quality of the plots in the original publication (below) was not ideal for reproduction, but we have made an effort to reproduce them as faithfully as possible, and the points are found in the "validation/validationData" directory.

The calculation took approx. 3 min to run on a single core laptop at the reference resolution (e.g. 500 cells). Reference solution and plots from this run are in the "validation/referencePlots" directory. Plots will be automatically created using the postProcess and createGraphs utilities for subsequent runs.

## Reference

```
Lai, S., Houim, R.W., Oran, E.S., 2018. Effects of particle size and density on dust dispersion behind a moving shock. Phys. Rev. Fluids 3, 064306. https://doi.org/10.1103/PhysRevFluids.3.064306


```


### Node Properties
- **Name**: turbulenceProperties.coal
- **Path**: blastEulerFoam/shockTube_polydisperse/constant/turbulenceProperties.coal
- **Source_file**: blastEulerFoam_shockTube_polydisperse.json
- **Case_id**: blastEulerFoam/shockTube_polydisperse
- **Original_id**: blastEulerFoam/shockTube_polydisperse/constant/turbulenceProperties.coal

- **Case Path**: blastFoam/triplePointShockInteration
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Triple-Point Shock Interaction

## Notes

This case is a Riemann problem with three states and two materials, where the the domain is split into three separate regions. Each region has an ideal gas equation of state. This case is designed to test the ability to propagate shocks over a multi-material regions and capture the generation of vorticity.

The case takes approximately 37 min to run on a 4 core desktop.

# Reacting Shock tube

## Notes

This is an illustrative case on how to use blastFoam with a simplechemistry. A single methane reaction is used, with two regions: The left containing 77% Nitrogen and 23& Oxygen at STP. The right region is pure methane at 10 atm and 500K. An Euler ODE solver for the chemistry reactions.

The calculation took approx. 30 s to run on a single core laptop at using 500 cells.

- **Description**: 
# Triple-Point Shock Interaction

## Notes

This case is a Riemann problem with three states and two materials, where the the domain is split into three separate regions. Each region has an ideal gas equation of state. This case is designed to test the ability to propagate shocks over a multi-material regions and capture the generation of vorticity.

The case takes approximately 37 min to run on a 4 core desktop.

- **Case Name**: flap
- **Case Path**: solids4Foam/flap
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Simple FSI flaps simulation

## Notes

This case is used to demonstrate the capability of blastFoam to handle fluid-structure interaction.

The blastFSIFoam solver is still under development and changing the case setup may result in crashes.

The case consists of a small c4 charge being detonated near a wall in 2 dimensions. The flap will deform when the blast wave reached it, and will effect how the wave moves past the flap. This is a modified version of the preCICE tutorial presented.

The calculation took approx. 2 hrs to run on a single core laptop at the reference resolution (e.g. 240x160 cells) and a maximum refinement level of 1.

- **Name**: blockMeshDict

# movingCone tutorial

## Notes

This case is a modified version of the OpenFOAM movingCone tutorial. The main modification is the use of the movingAdaptiveFvMesh dynamic mesh class. This allows for automatic mesh refinement in addition to the mesh movement.

If you would like to run this case in parallel, the Synthetik branch of OpenFOAM must be used due to a bug that causes moving, adaptive meshes to fail in parallel.

The case takes approximately 15 minutes on a four core desktop.

# Internal detonation tutorial

## Notes

This is a simple case to show an example case in a confined domain using an explosive material that has additional reactions occur after the initial detonation. The Miller afterburn model is used to additional energy (from heat of formation) in to the system. A small outlet is placed on the far side of the domain from the initial charge location.

The case uses adaptive mesh refinement, and took approximately 20 min to complete on a four core desktop.

- **Case Path**: blastFoam/mappedBuilding3D
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# mapped building 3D

## Notes

These cases are to show how to use the rotateFields utility to run a refined 1D/2D case to initialize a refined solution and then rotate to a 3D case. The sector (1D, serial) initialization is used by default, but the wedge (2D, parallel) initializtion can be used by running './Allwmake -wedge'. Both methods of initialization will write to the symbolicly linked 'axisymmetricCharge' folder.

The sector set of cases takes approximately 2 minutes to run the wedge case take approximately 10 minutes, both on a four core desktop.


### Node Properties
- **Name**: blockMeshDict
- **Path**: blastFoam/mappedBuilding3D/building3D/system/blockMeshDict
- **Source_file**: blastFoam_mappedBuilding3D.json
- **Case_id**: blastFoam/mappedBuilding3D

- **Description**: 
# Delayed detonation tutorial

## Notes

This is a simple case to show how to setup a case using delayed activation of points. A RDX charge is used with six activation point, with four of the six using a delayed start. A linear activation model is used, but the same setup of the detonation points can be applied to any of the other available models.

This case takes approximately 2.5 min to run on a single core desktop.

## Type: Variable (ID: blastFoam_triplePointShockInteration::Variable_startTime)

### Case Context
- **Case Name**: triplePointShockInteration
- **Case Path**: blastFoam/triplePointShockInteration
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Triple-Point Shock Interaction

## Notes

This case is a Riemann problem with three states and two materials, where the the domain is split into three separate regions. Each region has an ideal gas equation of state. This case is designed to test the ability to propagate shocks over a multi-material regions and capture the generation of vorticity.

The case takes approximately 37 min to run on a 4 core desktop.


### Node Properties
- **Name**: startTime
- **Type**: Parameter
- **Source_file**: blastFoam_triplePointShockInteration.json

This is an illustrative case on how to use blastFoam with a simplechemistry. A single methane reaction is used, with two regions: The left containing 77% Nitrogen and 23& Oxygen at STP. The right region is pure methane at 10 atm and 500K. An Euler ODE solver for the chemistry reactions.

The calculation took approx. 30 s to run on a single core laptop at using 500 cells.

# Triple-Point Shock Interaction

## Notes

This case is a Riemann problem with three states and two materials, where the the domain is split into three separate regions. Each region has an ideal gas equation of state. This case is designed to test the ability to propagate shocks over a multi-material regions and capture the generation of vorticity.

The case takes approximately 37 min to run on a 4 core desktop.

- **Description**: 
# Simple FSI flaps simulation

## Notes

This case is used to demonstrate the capability of blastFoam to handle fluid-structure interaction.

The blastFSIFoam solver is still under development and changing the case setup may result in crashes.

The case consists of a small c4 charge being detonated near a wall in 2 dimensions. The flap will deform when the blast wave reached it, and will effect how the wave moves past the flap. This is a modified version of the preCICE tutorial presented.

The calculation took approx. 2 hrs to run on a single core laptop at the reference resolution (e.g. 240x160 cells) and a maximum refinement level of 1.

- **Path**: solids4Foam/flap

This case is a modified version of the OpenFOAM movingCone tutorial. The main modification is the use of the movingAdaptiveFvMesh dynamic mesh class. This allows for automatic mesh refinement in addition to the mesh movement.

If you would like to run this case in parallel, the Synthetik branch of OpenFOAM must be used due to a bug that causes moving, adaptive meshes to fail in parallel.

# mapped building 3D

## Notes

These cases are to show how to use the rotateFields utility to run a refined 1D/2D case to initialize a refined solution and then rotate to a 3D case. The sector (1D, serial) initialization is used by default, but the wedge (2D, parallel) initializtion can be used by running './Allwmake -wedge'. Both methods of initialization will write to the symbolicly linked 'axisymmetricCharge' folder.

The sector set of cases takes approximately 2 minutes to run the wedge case take approximately 10 minutes, both on a four core desktop.

# Shock tube tabulated tutorial

## Notes

This case is used to show the ability to use a tabulated equation of state and thermodynamic model in the context of a simple shock tube. The provided tables were created using an ideal gas with a specific heat ratio of 1.4. Any table can be used as long as pressure and temperature tables are provided as a function of density and internal energy.

The case took approximately 10 s to complete on a single core desktop.


### Node Properties
- **Name**: shockTube_tabulated
- **Path**: blastFoam/shockTube_tabulated
- **Source_file**: blastFoam_shockTube_tabulated.json
- **Case_id**: blastFoam/shockTube_tabulated
- **Original_id**: blastFoam/shockTube_tabulated

detailed_results.query: Change obstacle patch naming and grouping rules for the internal detonation obstacle case.
detailed_results.raw_results:
Max iterations reached before finish. Returning best-effort results. --- Retrieved Case Content Information --- ## Type: File (ID: blastFoam_internalDetonation_internalDetonation_withObstacleAndGlass::blastFoam/internalDetonation/internalDetonation_withObstacleAndGlass/system/createPatchDict) ### Case Context - **Case Name**: internalDetonation_withObstacleAndGlass - **Case Path**: blastFoam/internalDetonation/internalDetonation_withObstacleAndGlass - **Solver**: N/A - **Description**: # Internal detonation with obstacle and glass tutorial ## Notes This case is used to illustrate how to both setup and use burst patches. The burst patches allow internal faces to act in one way prior to a condition being met, and internal faces after. In this case they are treated as walls prior to the overpressure or impulse reaching their burst condition (4e7 Pa and 1e4 Pa.s) respectively. It should be noted that the first two window burst due to the overpressure condition, while the final window bursts do to impulse. This can be seen due to the lag in time between when the primary pressure wave hits the final window and when it actually bursts. The mesh is created using the following procedure
  1. Base mesh is created with blockMesh
  2. Regions where windows will be placed are refined with setRefinedFields
  3. The outlet patch is added using createPatch
  4. The windows are created by adding baffles with the burstCyclicAMI patch type with createBaffles
  5. Obstructions uses cellSets selected from running setRefinedFields and running subsetMesh internalCells -patch walls
  6. The charge is the set using setRefinedFields
  7. Finally the case can be run with blastFoam
The case takes approximately 5 min to run on a single core desktop ### Node Properties - **Name**: createPatchDict - **Path**: blastFoam/internalDetonation/internalDetonation_withObstacleAndGlass/system/createPatchDict - **Source_file**: blastFoam_internalDetonation_internalDetonation_withObstacleAndGlass.json - **Case_id**: blastFoam/internalDetonation/internalDetonation_withObstacleAndGlass - **Original_id**: blastFoam/internalDetonation/internalDetonation_withObstacleAndGlass/system/createPatchDict

This is a simple case to show how to setup a case using multiple charges. A RDX charge is placed on top of a TNT charge. The activation coefficients are not specific to TNT and should not be used for actually calculations.

This case takes approximately 5 min to run on a single core desktop.

- **Case Path**: solids4Foam/flap
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Simple FSI flaps simulation

## Notes

This case is used to demonstrate the capability of blastFoam to handle fluid-structure interaction.

The blastFSIFoam solver is still under development and changing the case setup may result in crashes.

The case consists of a small c4 charge being detonated near a wall in 2 dimensions. The flap will deform when the blast wave reached it, and will effect how the wave moves past the flap. This is a modified version of the preCICE tutorial presented.

The calculation took approx. 2 hrs to run on a single core laptop at the reference resolution (e.g. 240x160 cells) and a maximum refinement level of 1.



### Node Properties
- **Name**: setFieldsDict
- **Path**: solids4Foam/flap/system/fluid/setFieldsDict
- **Source_file**: solids4Foam_flap.json
- **Case_id**: solids4Foam/flap
- **Original_id**: solids4Foam/flap/system/fluid/setFieldsDict

- **Description**: 
# Internal detonation tutorial

## Notes

This is a simple case to show an example case in a confined domain using an explosive material that has additional reactions occur after the initial detonation. The Miller afterburn model is used to additional energy (from heat of formation) in to the system. A small outlet is placed on the far side of the domain from the initial charge location.

The case uses adaptive mesh refinement, and took approximately 20 min to complete on a four core desktop.

- **Case Path**: blastFoam/mappedBuilding3D

- **Description**: 
# mapped building 3D

## Notes

These cases are to show how to use the rotateFields utility to run a refined 1D/2D case to initialize a refined solution and then rotate to a 3D case. The sector (1D, serial) initialization is used by default, but the wedge (2D, parallel) initializtion can be used by running './Allwmake -wedge'. Both methods of initialization will write to the symbolicly linked 'axisymmetricCharge' folder.

The sector set of cases takes approximately 2 minutes to run the wedge case take approximately 10 minutes, both on a four core desktop.


### Node Properties
- **Name**: setFieldsDict
- **Path**: blastFoam/mappedBuilding3D/building3D/system/setFieldsDict
- **Source_file**: blastFoam_mappedBuilding3D.json
- **Case_id**: blastFoam/mappedBuilding3D

- **Case Path**: blastFoam/twoChargeDetonation
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Two charge detonation tutorial

## Notes

This is a simple case to show how to setup a case using multiple charges. A RDX charge is placed on top of a TNT charge. The top charge is activated when the simulation starts, and the TNT uses a pressure based activation model to simulate how the material activates as the pressure wave passes through the material. The activation coefficients are not specific to TNT and should not be used for actually calculations.

This case takes approximately 5 min to run on a single core desktop.

- **Path**: blastFoam/twoChargeDetonation/system/controlDict

- **Description**: 
# Reacting Shock tube

## Notes

This is an illustrative case on how to use blastFoam with a simplechemistry. A single methane reaction is used, with two regions: The left containing 77% Nitrogen and 23& Oxygen at STP. The right region is pure methane at 10 atm and 500K. An Euler ODE solver for the chemistry reactions.

The calculation took approx. 30 s to run on a single core laptop at using 500 cells.

- **Description**: 
# Shock tube tabulated tutorial

## Notes

This case is used to show the ability to use a tabulated equation of state and thermodynamic model in the context of a simple shock tube. The provided tables were created using an ideal gas with a specific heat ratio of 1.4. Any table can be used as long as pressure and temperature tables are provided as a function of density and internal energy.

The case took approximately 10 s to complete on a single core desktop.

### Case Context
- **Case Name**: flap
- **Case Path**: blastFSIFoam/flap
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# FSI flap simulation

## Notes

This case is used to demonstrate the capability of blastFoam to handle fluid-structure interaction.

The blastFSIFoam solver is still under development and changing the case setup may result in crashes.

The case consists of a small c4 charge being detonated near a wall in 2 dimensions. The flap will deform when the blast wave reached it, and will effect how the wave moves past the flap. This is a modified version of the preCICE tutorial presented.

The calculation took \~6 minutes to run on a four-core desktop.



### Node Properties
- **Name**: thermophysicalProperties
- **Path**: blastFSIFoam/flap/constant/flap/thermophysicalProperties
- **Source_file**: blastFSIFoam_flap.json
- **Case_id**: blastFSIFoam/flap
- **Original_id**: blastFSIFoam/flap/constant/flap/thermophysicalProperties

- **Case Path**: blastFoam/reacting
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Reacting Shock tube

## Notes

This is an illustrative case on how to use blastFoam with a simplechemistry. A single methane reaction is used, with two regions: The left containing 77% Nitrogen and 23& Oxygen at STP. The right region is pure methane at 10 atm and 500K. An Euler ODE solver for the chemistry reactions.

The calculation took approx. 30 s to run on a single core laptop at using 500 cells.

This is an illustrative case on how to use blastFoam with a simplechemistry. A single methane reaction is used, with two regions: The left containing 77% Nitrogen and 23& Oxygen at STP. The right region is pure methane at 10 atm and 500K. An Euler ODE solver for the chemistry reactions.

The calculation took approx. 30 s to run on a single core laptop at using 500 cells.

- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# blastParcelFoam forwardStep tutorial

## Notes

This is a modified version of the standard forwardStep tutorial from OpenFOAM. The case has been modified to include inertial, heated, Lagrangian particles.

Two methods to view the lagrangian particles exist: The standard OpenFOAM paraview reader can be used (*.OpenFOAM extension), but requires the case to first be reconstructed using reconstructPar. The particles can the be viewed using the "Glyph" filter. The second method is to use the "convertLagrangianPositions" to modifiy the outputs of the solver so that the standard paraview OpenFOAM (*.foam extension) can read the lagrangian files without. If you would like to restart the simulation after using this method, you must use the "-revert" flag so that OpenFOAM can correclty read in the lagrangian fields. The second method is used for this case.

The case takes approximately 30 min to run on a four-core desktop.




### Node Properties
- **Name**: cloudProperties
- **Path**: blastParcelFoam/forwardStep/constant/cloudProperties
- **Source_file**: blastParcelFoam_forwardStep.json
- **Case_id**: blastParcelFoam/forwardStep
- **Original_id**: blastParcelFoam/forwardStep/constant/cloudProperties

# Triple-Point Shock Interaction

## Notes

This case is a Riemann problem with three states and two materials, where the the domain is split into three separate regions. Each region has an ideal gas equation of state. This case is designed to test the ability to propagate shocks over a multi-material regions and capture the generation of vorticity.

The case takes approximately 37 min to run on a 4 core desktop.

### Case Context
- **Case Name**: flap
- **Case Path**: solids4Foam/flap
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Simple FSI flaps simulation

## Notes

This case is used to demonstrate the capability of blastFoam to handle fluid-structure interaction.

The blastFSIFoam solver is still under development and changing the case setup may result in crashes.

The case consists of a small c4 charge being detonated near a wall in 2 dimensions. The flap will deform when the blast wave reached it, and will effect how the wave moves past the flap. This is a modified version of the preCICE tutorial presented.

The calculation took approx. 2 hrs to run on a single core laptop at the reference resolution (e.g. 240x160 cells) and a maximum refinement level of 1.

- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Shock tube tabulated tutorial

## Notes

This case is used to show the ability to use a tabulated equation of state and thermodynamic model in the context of a simple shock tube. The provided tables were created using an ideal gas with a specific heat ratio of 1.4. Any table can be used as long as pressure and temperature tables are provided as a function of density and internal energy.

The case took approximately 10 s to complete on a single core desktop.

# Reacting Shock tube

## Notes

This is an illustrative case on how to use blastFoam with a simplechemistry. A single methane reaction is used, with two regions: The left containing 77% Nitrogen and 23& Oxygen at STP. The right region is pure methane at 10 atm and 500K. An Euler ODE solver for the chemistry reactions.

The calculation took approx. 30 s to run on a single core laptop at using 500 cells.

- **Description**: 
# movingCone tutorial

## Notes

This case is a modified version of the OpenFOAM movingCone tutorial. The main modification is the use of the movingAdaptiveFvMesh dynamic mesh class. This allows for automatic mesh refinement in addition to the mesh movement.

If you would like to run this case in parallel, the Synthetik branch of OpenFOAM must be used due to a bug that causes moving, adaptive meshes to fail in parallel.

The case takes approximately 15 minutes on a four core desktop.


### Node Properties
- **Name**: fvSchemes
- **Path**: blastFoam/movingCone/system/fvSchemes
- **Source_file**: blastFoam_movingCone.json
- **Case_id**: blastFoam/movingCone
- **Original_id**: blastFoam/movingCone/system/fvSchemes

- **Description**: 
# FSI flap simulation

## Notes

This case is used to demonstrate the capability of blastFoam to handle fluid-structure interaction.

The blastFSIFoam solver is still under development and changing the case setup may result in crashes.

The case consists of a small c4 charge being detonated near a wall in 2 dimensions. The flap will deform when the blast wave reached it, and will effect how the wave moves past the flap. This is a modified version of the preCICE tutorial presented.

The calculation took \~6 minutes to run on a four-core desktop.

- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Internal detonation tutorial

## Notes

This is a simple case to show an example case in a confined domain using an explosive material that has additional reactions occur after the initial detonation. The Miller afterburn model is used to additional energy (from heat of formation) in to the system. A small outlet is placed on the far side of the domain from the initial charge location.

The case uses adaptive mesh refinement, and took approximately 20 min to complete on a four core desktop.




### Node Properties
- **Name**: fvSchemes
- **Path**: blastFoam/internalDetonation/internalDetonation/system/fvSchemes
- **Source_file**: blastFoam_internalDetonation_internalDetonation.json

## Type: File (ID: blastEulerFoam_shockTube_polydisperse::blastEulerFoam/shockTube_polydisperse/system/controlDict)

### Case Context
- **Case Name**: shockTube_polydisperse
- **Case Path**: blastEulerFoam/shockTube_polydisperse
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Validation of polydispserse granular

## Notes

This case uses six granular phases within a continuous air phase. This a dilute simulation where packing of particles does no occur. The continuous gas phase is represented by air. Five of the particles phases are used to represent a continuous size distribution with a density 2700 kg/m^3 and a coefficient of restitution of 0.9. The last granular phase is that of coal with a density of 1300 kg/m^3, coefficient of restitution of 0.9, and a diameter of 10e-6 m.

Original plots were digitized using WebPlotDigitizer: https://github.com/ankitrohatgi/WebPlotDigitizer/releases The quality of the plots in the original publication (below) was not ideal for reproduction, but we have made an effort to reproduce them as faithfully as possible, and the points are found in the "validation/validationData" directory.

The calculation took approx. 3 min to run on a single core laptop at the reference resolution (e.g. 500 cells). Reference solution and plots from this run are in the "validation/referencePlots" directory. Plots will be automatically created using the postProcess and createGraphs utilities for subsequent runs.

## Reference

```
Lai, S., Houim, R.W., Oran, E.S., 2018. Effects of particle size and density on dust dispersion behind a moving shock. Phys. Rev. Fluids 3, 064306. https://doi.org/10.1103/PhysRevFluids.3.064306


```


### Node Properties
- **Name**: controlDict
- **Path**: blastEulerFoam/shockTube_polydisperse/system/controlDict
- **Source_file**: blastEulerFoam_shockTube_polydisperse.json
- **Case_id**: blastEulerFoam/shockTube_polydisperse
- **Original_id**: blastEulerFoam/shockTube_polydisperse/system/controlDict

# Validation of polydispserse granular

## Notes

This case uses six granular phases within a continuous air phase. This a dilute simulation where packing of particles does no occur. The continuous gas phase is represented by air. Five of the particles phases are used to represent a continuous size distribution with a density 2700 kg/m^3 and a coefficient of restitution of 0.9. The last granular phase is that of coal with a density of 1300 kg/m^3, coefficient of restitution of 0.9, and a diameter of 10e-6 m.

Original plots were digitized using WebPlotDigitizer: https://github.com/ankitrohatgi/WebPlotDigitizer/releases The quality of the plots in the original publication (below) was not ideal for reproduction, but we have made an effort to reproduce them as faithfully as possible, and the points are found in the "validation/validationData" directory.

The calculation took approx. 3 min to run on a single core laptop at the reference resolution (e.g. 500 cells). ## Type: File (ID: blastEulerFoam_shockTube_polydisperse::blastEulerFoam/shockTube_polydisperse/constant/turbulenceProperties.particles2)

### Case Context
- **Case Name**: shockTube_polydisperse
- **Case Path**: blastEulerFoam/shockTube_polydisperse
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Validation of polydispserse granular

## Notes

This case uses six granular phases within a continuous air phase. This a dilute simulation where packing of particles does no occur. The continuous gas phase is represented by air. Five of the particles phases are used to represent a continuous size distribution with a density 2700 kg/m^3 and a coefficient of restitution of 0.9. The last granular phase is that of coal with a density of 1300 kg/m^3, coefficient of restitution of 0.9, and a diameter of 10e-6 m.

Original plots were digitized using WebPlotDigitizer: https://github.com/ankitrohatgi/WebPlotDigitizer/releases The quality of the plots in the original publication (below) was not ideal for reproduction, but we have made an effort to reproduce them as faithfully as possible, and the points are found in the "validation/validationData" directory.

The calculation took approx. 3 min to run on a single core laptop at the reference resolution (e.g. 500 cells). Reference solution and plots from this run are in the "validation/referencePlots" directory. Plots will be automatically created using the postProcess and createGraphs utilities for subsequent runs.

## Reference

```
Lai, S., Houim, R.W., Oran, E.S., 2018. Effects of particle size and density on dust dispersion behind a moving shock. Phys. Rev. Fluids 3, 064306. https://doi.org/10.1103/PhysRevFluids.3.064306


```

## Type: File (ID: solids4Foam_flap::solids4Foam/flap/constant/fluid/turbulenceProperties)

### Case Context
- **Case Name**: flap
- **Case Path**: solids4Foam/flap
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Simple FSI flaps simulation

## Notes

This case is used to demonstrate the capability of blastFoam to handle fluid-structure interaction.

The blastFSIFoam solver is still under development and changing the case setup may result in crashes.

The case consists of a small c4 charge being detonated near a wall in 2 dimensions. The flap will deform when the blast wave reached it, and will effect how the wave moves past the flap. This is a modified version of the preCICE tutorial presented.

The calculation took approx. 2 hrs to run on a single core laptop at the reference resolution (e.g. 240x160 cells) and a maximum refinement level of 1.

- **Description**: 
# movingCone tutorial

## Notes

This case is a modified version of the OpenFOAM movingCone tutorial. The main modification is the use of the movingAdaptiveFvMesh dynamic mesh class. This allows for automatic mesh refinement in addition to the mesh movement.

If you would like to run this case in parallel, the Synthetik branch of OpenFOAM must be used due to a bug that causes moving, adaptive meshes to fail in parallel.

The case takes approximately 15 minutes on a four core desktop.

# Reacting Shock tube

## Notes

This is an illustrative case on how to use blastFoam with a simplechemistry. A single methane reaction is used, with two regions: The left containing 77% Nitrogen and 23& Oxygen at STP. The right region is pure methane at 10 atm and 500K. An Euler ODE solver for the chemistry reactions.

The calculation took approx. 30 s to run on a single core laptop at using 500 cells.

- **Description**: 
# Triple-Point Shock Interaction

## Notes

This case is a Riemann problem with three states and two materials, where the the domain is split into three separate regions. Each region has an ideal gas equation of state. This case is designed to test the ability to propagate shocks over a multi-material regions and capture the generation of vorticity.

The case takes approximately 37 min to run on a 4 core desktop.

- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Simple FSI flaps simulation

## Notes

This case is used to demonstrate the capability of blastFoam to handle fluid-structure interaction.

The blastFSIFoam solver is still under development and changing the case setup may result in crashes.

The case consists of a small c4 charge being detonated near a wall in 2 dimensions. The flap will deform when the blast wave reached it, and will effect how the wave moves past the flap. This is a modified version of the preCICE tutorial presented.

The calculation took approx. 2 hrs to run on a single core laptop at the reference resolution (e.g. 240x160 cells) and a maximum refinement level of 1.



### Node Properties
- **Name**: fvSolution
- **Path**: solids4Foam/flap/system/fluid/fvSolution
- **Source_file**: solids4Foam_flap.json
- **Case_id**: solids4Foam/flap
- **Original_id**: solids4Foam/flap/system/fluid/fvSolution

### Case Context
- **Case Name**: movingCone
- **Case Path**: blastFoam/movingCone
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# movingCone tutorial

## Notes

This case is a modified version of the OpenFOAM movingCone tutorial. The main modification is the use of the movingAdaptiveFvMesh dynamic mesh class. This allows for automatic mesh refinement in addition to the mesh movement.

If you would like to run this case in parallel, the Synthetik branch of OpenFOAM must be used due to a bug that causes moving, adaptive meshes to fail in parallel.

The case takes approximately 15 minutes on a four core desktop.


### Node Properties
- **Name**: fvSchemes
- **Path**: blastFoam/movingCone/system/fvSchemes

# Building 3-D with FSI tutorial

## Notes

This case is designed to show a simple 3-D case setup using an STL file with snappyHexMesh and adaptive mesh refinement. The a c-4 charge placed inside a L-Shaped building. The c-4 is activated using the linear activation model. This cases also uses fluid structure interaction where the pressure is used to deform the solid wall and the solids use a linear-elastic material model.

The case takes approximately 6 min to run on a four-core desktop.

- **Case Path**: blastFoam/reacting
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Reacting Shock tube

## Notes

This is an illustrative case on how to use blastFoam with a simplechemistry. A single methane reaction is used, with two regions: The left containing 77% Nitrogen and 23& Oxygen at STP. The right region is pure methane at 10 atm and 500K. An Euler ODE solver for the chemistry reactions.

The calculation took approx. 30 s to run on a single core laptop at using 500 cells.

- **Case Path**: blastFoam/triplePointShockInteration
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Triple-Point Shock Interaction

## Notes

This case is a Riemann problem with three states and two materials, where the the domain is split into three separate regions. Each region has an ideal gas equation of state. This case is designed to test the ability to propagate shocks over a multi-material regions and capture the generation of vorticity.

The case takes approximately 37 min to run on a 4 core desktop.

# Triple-Point Shock Interaction

## Notes

This case is a Riemann problem with three states and two materials, where the the domain is split into three separate regions. Each region has an ideal gas equation of state. This case is designed to test the ability to propagate shocks over a multi-material regions and capture the generation of vorticity.

The case takes approximately 37 min to run on a 4 core desktop.

- **Case Path**: solids4Foam/flap
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Simple FSI flaps simulation

## Notes

This case is used to demonstrate the capability of blastFoam to handle fluid-structure interaction.

The blastFSIFoam solver is still under development and changing the case setup may result in crashes.

The case consists of a small c4 charge being detonated near a wall in 2 dimensions. The flap will deform when the blast wave reached it, and will effect how the wave moves past the flap. This is a modified version of the preCICE tutorial presented.

The calculation took approx. 2 hrs to run on a single core laptop at the reference resolution (e.g. 240x160 cells) and a maximum refinement level of 1.



### Node Properties
- **Name**: fvSchemes
- **Path**: solids4Foam/flap/system/fvSchemes

- **Description**: 
# movingCone tutorial

## Notes

This case is a modified version of the OpenFOAM movingCone tutorial. The main modification is the use of the movingAdaptiveFvMesh dynamic mesh class. This allows for automatic mesh refinement in addition to the mesh movement.

If you would like to run this case in parallel, the Synthetik branch of OpenFOAM must be used due to a bug that causes moving, adaptive meshes to fail in parallel.

The case takes approximately 15 minutes on a four core desktop.

# FSI flap simulation

## Notes

This case is used to demonstrate the capability of blastFoam to handle fluid-structure interaction.

The blastFSIFoam solver is still under development and changing the case setup may result in crashes.

The case consists of a small c4 charge being detonated near a wall in 2 dimensions. The flap will deform when the blast wave reached it, and will effect how the wave moves past the flap. This is a modified version of the preCICE tutorial presented.

The calculation took \~6 minutes to run on a four-core desktop.

# Reacting Shock tube

## Notes

This is an illustrative case on how to use blastFoam with a simplechemistry. A single methane reaction is used, with two regions: The left containing 77% Nitrogen and 23& Oxygen at STP. The right region is pure methane at 10 atm and 500K. An Euler ODE solver for the chemistry reactions.

The calculation took approx. 30 s to run on a single core laptop at using 500 cells.

# Triple-Point Shock Interaction

## Notes

This case is a Riemann problem with three states and two materials, where the the domain is split into three separate regions. Each region has an ideal gas equation of state. This case is designed to test the ability to propagate shocks over a multi-material regions and capture the generation of vorticity.

The case takes approximately 37 min to run on a 4 core desktop.

# movingCone tutorial

## Notes

This case is a modified version of the OpenFOAM movingCone tutorial. The main modification is the use of the movingAdaptiveFvMesh dynamic mesh class. This allows for automatic mesh refinement in addition to the mesh movement.

If you would like to run this case in parallel, the Synthetik branch of OpenFOAM must be used due to a bug that causes moving, adaptive meshes to fail in parallel.

The case takes approximately 15 minutes on a four core desktop.

# Reacting Shock tube

## Notes

This is an illustrative case on how to use blastFoam with a simplechemistry. A single methane reaction is used, with two regions: The left containing 77% Nitrogen and 23& Oxygen at STP. The right region is pure methane at 10 atm and 500K. An Euler ODE solver for the chemistry reactions.

The calculation took approx. 30 s to run on a single core laptop at using 500 cells.

# Triple-Point Shock Interaction

## Notes

This case is a Riemann problem with three states and two materials, where the the domain is split into three separate regions. Each region has an ideal gas equation of state. This case is designed to test the ability to propagate shocks over a multi-material regions and capture the generation of vorticity.

The case takes approximately 37 min to run on a 4 core desktop.

## Notes

This case is a Riemann problem with three states and two materials, where the the domain is split into three separate regions. Each region has an ideal gas equation of state. The case takes approximately 37 min to run on a 4 core desktop.

# Reacting Shock tube

## Notes

This is an illustrative case on how to use blastFoam with a simplechemistry. A single methane reaction is used, with two regions: The left containing 77% Nitrogen and 23& Oxygen at STP. The right region is pure methane at 10 atm and 500K. An Euler ODE solver for the chemistry reactions.

The calculation took approx. 30 s to run on a single core laptop at using 500 cells.

- **Case Path**: blastFoam/internalDetonation/internalDetonation
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Internal detonation tutorial

## Notes

This is a simple case to show an example case in a confined domain using an explosive material that has additional reactions occur after the initial detonation. The Miller afterburn model is used to additional energy (from heat of formation) in to the system. A small outlet is placed on the far side of the domain from the initial charge location.

The case uses adaptive mesh refinement, and took approximately 20 min to complete on a four core desktop.

- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Two charge detonation tutorial

## Notes

This is a simple case to show how to setup a case using multiple charges. A RDX charge is placed on top of a TNT charge. The top charge is activated when the simulation starts, and the TNT uses a pressure based activation model to simulate how the material activates as the pressure wave passes through the material. The activation coefficients are not specific to TNT and should not be used for actually calculations.

This case takes approximately 5 min to run on a single core desktop.

- **Case Path**: blastFoam/twoChargeDetonation
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Two charge detonation tutorial

## Notes

This is a simple case to show how to setup a case using multiple charges. A RDX charge is placed on top of a TNT charge. The top charge is activated when the simulation starts, and the TNT uses a pressure based activation model to simulate how the material activates as the pressure wave passes through the material. The activation coefficients are not specific to TNT and should not be used for actually calculations.

This case takes approximately 5 min to run on a single core desktop.

- **Path**: blastFoam/twoChargeDetonation/0/T.orig

- **Case Path**: blastFoam/triplePointShockInteration

- **Description**: 
# Triple-Point Shock Interaction

## Notes

This case is a Riemann problem with three states and two materials, where the the domain is split into three separate regions. Each region has an ideal gas equation of state. This case is designed to test the ability to propagate shocks over a multi-material regions and capture the generation of vorticity.

The case takes approximately 37 min to run on a 4 core desktop.

## Type: File (ID: solids4Foam_flap::solids4Foam/flap/system/controlDict)

### Case Context
- **Case Name**: flap
- **Case Path**: solids4Foam/flap
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Simple FSI flaps simulation

## Notes

This case is used to demonstrate the capability of blastFoam to handle fluid-structure interaction.

The blastFSIFoam solver is still under development and changing the case setup may result in crashes.

The case consists of a small c4 charge being detonated near a wall in 2 dimensions. The flap will deform when the blast wave reached it, and will effect how the wave moves past the flap. This is a modified version of the preCICE tutorial presented.

The calculation took approx. 2 hrs to run on a single core laptop at the reference resolution (e.g. 240x160 cells) and a maximum refinement level of 1.



### Node Properties
- **Name**: controlDict
- **Path**: solids4Foam/flap/system/controlDict
- **Source_file**: solids4Foam_flap.json
- **Case_id**: solids4Foam/flap

- **Case Path**: blastFoam/movingCone
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# movingCone tutorial

## Notes

This case is a modified version of the OpenFOAM movingCone tutorial. The main modification is the use of the movingAdaptiveFvMesh dynamic mesh class. This allows for automatic mesh refinement in addition to the mesh movement.

If you would like to run this case in parallel, the Synthetik branch of OpenFOAM must be used due to a bug that causes moving, adaptive meshes to fail in parallel.

The case takes approximately 15 minutes on a four core desktop.


### Node Properties
- **Name**: controlDict
- **Path**: blastFoam/movingCone/system/controlDict

- **Case Path**: blastFoam/internalDetonation/internalDetonation
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Internal detonation tutorial

## Notes

This is a simple case to show an example case in a confined domain using an explosive material that has additional reactions occur after the initial detonation. The Miller afterburn model is used to additional energy (from heat of formation) in to the system. A small outlet is placed on the far side of the domain from the initial charge location.

The case uses adaptive mesh refinement, and took approximately 20 min to complete on a four core desktop.

- **Name**: internalDetonation
- **Path**: blastFoam/internalDetonation/internalDetonation
- **Source_file**: blastFoam_internalDetonation_internalDetonation.json

- **Case Path**: blastFoam/mappedBuilding3D
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# mapped building 3D

## Notes

These cases are to show how to use the rotateFields utility to run a refined 1D/2D case to initialize a refined solution and then rotate to a 3D case. The sector (1D, serial) initialization is used by default, but the wedge (2D, parallel) initializtion can be used by running './Allwmake -wedge'. Both methods of initialization will write to the symbolicly linked 'axisymmetricCharge' folder.

The sector set of cases takes approximately 2 minutes to run the wedge case take approximately 10 minutes, both on a four core desktop.


### Node Properties
- **Name**: mappedBuilding3D
- **Path**: blastFoam/mappedBuilding3D
- **Source_file**: blastFoam_mappedBuilding3D.json
- **Case_id**: blastFoam/mappedBuilding3D
